content.assessment: Removing only the age-10 entry point shortens the selection funnel by one stage but explicitly preserves the age-12 and age-14 entry points and the overall selective-school share. This is a partial institutional adjustment: it changes when the earliest sorting occurs but leaves the governance and access mechanisms for the remaining two tracks, and the church/private exemption structure, entirely unchanged.
opportunities: Could serve as an institutionally testable increment to assess whether narrower reform reduces sorting pressure before considering further stages
strengths: Removes the earliest and youngest selection point (age 10-11), aligning that portion of the system closer to OECD-typical later tracking
threats: Because two selection points remain, demand and sorting pressure may simply relocate to the age-12 entry point rather than being reduced system-wide, leaving overall segregation levels largely unaddressed
weaknesses: Retains both remaining entry points (age 12 and 14), so the bulk of the current selective-school architecture (85% share) and its associated segregation patterns remain structurally intact
Does not address church/private-school exemptions from standard admissions rules, an unchanged governance channel
